inputs.prompt: Subject: Freelance writing — specifically the business side, not craft or technique. Reader: Mid-career freelance writers and journalists who are good at writing but bad at the business. Motivation: I spent 10 years freelancing and learned every lesson the hard way. I wanted a resource that treated writing as a profession, not an art form. Competitors: The Rebooting (media business focus, not freelance-specific), Study Hall (community-focused, less analytical), Who Pays Writers (data only, no analysis).

Subject: Book recommendations. Reader: People who read a lot and want to read better. Motivation: There are too many book newsletters that just list new releases. Competitors: The Millions, LitHub newsletter.

Subject: European housing policy — specifically the cross-border view that national media cannot provide. Reader: EU policy professionals, housing researchers, journalists who need to understand housing as a European issue. Motivation: Every country covers its own housing crisis in isolation. Nobody connects the dots across borders. Competitors: There are no direct competitors doing pan-European housing policy in newsletter form. The closest are national-level housing policy publications (Inside Housing UK, Immobilien Zeitung Germany) but they are country-specific.

inputs.description: Edge case — unfocused subject with no clear niche
inputs.prompt: Subject: Everything about dogs — breed guides, health, training, behavior, product reviews, rescue stories, news, opinion, humor. Reader: Anyone who has a dog or wants one. Motivation: I just really love dogs and want to write about them. Competitors: The Bark, AKC newsletter, Dogster, BarkPost, countless Instagram accounts. Background: I have owned three dogs. Refuse: I'm not sure — I don't want to limit myself too much.

inputs.description: Non-English cultural context — Gulf states art scene
inputs.prompt: Subject: The contemporary art scene in the Gulf states — exhibitions, gallery openings, artist profiles, auction results, and cultural policy in the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, and Oman. Reader: Collectors, curators, gallerists, and culturally engaged professionals across the Middle East and diaspora communities. Motivation: Western art media covers the Gulf only during Art Dubai week or when a record auction price hits. There is no consistent English-language source covering the region's art ecosystem week to week. Competitors: The Art Newspaper (global, rarely covers the Gulf outside major events), Canvas Magazine (print, irregular schedule). Refuse: I won't cover art purely as a luxury market story. The collector-as-investor angle dominates Western coverage and misrepresents what is happening.

inputs.description: Different media format — undecided between newsletter and podcast
inputs.prompt: Subject: Climate adaptation infrastructure — flood barriers, urban heat mitigation, managed retreat, building codes. Reader: Local government officials, urban planners, and climate adaptation professionals who need practical case studies, not climate doom content. Motivation: Climate journalism focuses on mitigation and catastrophe. Nobody covers the actual engineering and policy of adaptation in a way that municipal officials can use. Competitors: Carbon Brief (mitigation-focused), Heatmap (consumer-facing), FEMA newsletters (too bureaucratic). I want to position this as a paid Substack newsletter, but also considering launching it as a podcast-first format — I haven't decided yet.

Subject: its basicaly about stuff thats interesting, like science and tech and culture and maybe politics sometimes. Reader: smart people who want to think about things more deeply? Motivation: I think I have good taste in what to read and i want to share that. Competitors: Ive seen some newsletters but i dont remeber the names. I think theres one called brain pickings or something?

inputs.description: Stress scenario — urgent launch to fill coverage gap
inputs.prompt: Subject: Public education funding and school board politics in our metro area. Reader: Parents, teachers, and local taxpayers who vote on school board elections and bond measures but can't parse the district's budget documents. Motivation: Our local newspaper just laid off its education reporter last week. There is now zero dedicated coverage of a school district with 85,000 students and a $1.2 billion annual budget. I want to launch within two weeks to fill the gap before the November bond election. Competitors: The now-defunct education desk at the local paper; one parent-run Facebook group that shares information but does no original reporting. Background: I'm a former education reporter with 7 years' experience. Refuse: I won't take advertising from any organization that has business before the school board. I won't cover individual teacher disputes or parent complaints unless they reflect systemic issues.
